posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
I understand and appreciate the concept of Bourgogne rouge being a cellar defender – a wine to open early while waiting for the village, 1er cru and GCs to integrate/mature to full complexity. But I’m curious what exactly allows them to be enjoyed earlier than their most exalted stablemates? Why don’t they need to slumber as well if they are coming from grapes grown under similar conditions/vintage characteristics?

Is it merely a function of them being made from “lesser” grapes that somehow don’t need as much time to be enjoyed? Or is it more a function of the winemaking? In the vineyard or in the cellar, does the vintner treat the grapes that will become the Bourgogne in a way that will allow the wine to be enjoyed on release? Or is it more that he/she consciously employs practices to the classified wines that essentially demands significant time before opening? Or a combination of both?

I know it’s a bit of a newb question. But a really nice bottle of Fourrier 2013 Bourgogne Rouge got me thinking about it last night. There is a lot of enjoyment and value in this $25 bottle of wine. Fourrier is obviously a winemaker of great precision and care, so it’s nice to see him put effort into this wine. It’s stony, transparent, flecked with iron notes. But that trademark gossamer mouthfeel is where it really shines. A modest wine (in the best sense of the word) – but the vintage and winemaker absolutely shine through.

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked: There’s a reason the vineyards are categorized in Burgundy. It’s not a perfect system but there is a marked difference between vineyards and, yes, some vineyards are lesser even if they are still capable of producing very nice wines especially within the hands of excellent vintners.
I had this one last week and wondered if it or a portion of it was from declassified fruit. That aside, this was probably the most polished bourgogne I can remember having drank.

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked: Some Bourgognes are higher level fruit notched down. Expensive decision to make. Don’t know Fourrier’s details regarding this wine. Most Bourgognes I believe are entirely or largely from more humble vineyard plots.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Although some plots are less humble than others.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This raises a more general question about how strongly longevity and hierarchy correlate in Burgundy. While there obviously is a correlation, and a pretty strong one too, I would suggest that it is sometimes assumed too uncritically. On the one hand, it’s easy to imagine a Clos St Jaques or Cros Parantoux having a longer life expectancy than many renditions of Latricieres or Charmes Chambertin; and I can even think of Vosne Beaux Monts that seem likely to outlive Richebourgs of the same vintage. On the other, I think there must be few renditions of Vosne Chaumes that are likely to outlive, say, Mugneret Gibourg’s Vosne AOC.

In addition to exceptions to the rule, it’s certainly the case—in some cellars and vintages—that the village wines don’t take that much less time to really open up than the 1ers, and 1ers don’t take that much less time to open up than the grand crus. That is not to say that the higher classifications will not carry on going strong when the village wines have begun to run out of gas, but rather to suggest that the best village wines are no more good to go at age five than 1er or grand crus are.

Obviously, wine making is going to be a hugely important factor in this, which brings us back to the original question, to which the answer, I think, is that it would be possible to make an extremely structured, long-lived Bourgogne, but that doing so would somewhat defeat the purpose of Bourgogne and is unlikely to be justified or facilitated by the intrinsic character of the site.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Matthew King:

Is it merely a function of them being made from “lesser” grapes that somehow don’t need as much time to be enjoyed? Or is it more a function of the winemaking? In the vineyard or in the cellar, does the vintner treat the grapes that will become the Bourgogne in a way that will allow the wine to be enjoyed on release? Or is it more that he/she consciously employs practices to the classified wines that essentially demands significant time before opening? Or a combination of both?

For the lesser vineyards, the grapes may spend less time on the skins and thus have less tannin. The wine might be aged in steel tanks instead of wood at least part of the time, which would make it fruitier. And a Bourgogne is unlikely to spent any time in new oak barrels. Also, they’re usually released sooner, so they may retain more of their initial fruit.

By contrast, if you’re making a premier or grand cru, you’ll feel safe making a denser wine with more tannin and new oak that might not show as well young because you expect a payoff from this style down the road.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: Some estates treat all of their wines pretty much the same, other than varying the amount of new oak, perhaps for simplicity. Others clearly don’t. (It would be interesting to know how they vary their handling of Bourgognes and perhaps village wines to have them drink earlier.) I have one bottle left from a case of the 1993 Bourgogne Rouge from deMontille. As of the next to the last bottle, it’s still not ready to drink. Very much like all of the rest of their wines from that era.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Matthew,

Interesting question…a slight twist on the usual one of whether a villages is worth aging as much as 1er or grand crus. (IMO, they are, with realistic, slightly lower expectations of the heights they can aspire to-- depending on the producer and its stable.) I don’t think that bourgognes fit into that analysis/ are cellar “defenders”. The appellation Bourgogne is just too diverse to be such a “defender”, though perhaps a couple of examples might qualify.

Bourgognes are all over the place at the domaines…and negociants in Burgundy. Some are purposefully made in a different style (often the larger operations like the negoces) to be early drinking and consistently inoffensive, not for aging. Some are made well, but use the dregs of the other vineyards for their grapes in their Bourgognes. Others make them all in steel/fiberglass, seeing no oak, new or old for softening, and see no elevage (barrel aging to allow that to happen). Other domains, in my experience visiting them, made their Bourgognes in different facilities and bottled them within six months of a vintage. Others (and these were good estates that made lovely “higher” stuff used carbonic maceration to make succulent wines a la Beaujolais. And, in fairness, of all the visits to all the really good estates I’ve made over the years, at many of them, they never ever offered a Bourgogne for tasting, even when I requested it, which I often did (So, I’ve never tasted a Bourgogne at Rousseau, Hubert Lignier, d’Angerville or Rouget/Jayer for example). They were gone and sold and somewhat “unrelated” to the rest of their mix. At the tip of the bottom of the pyramid, of course, are the lieux dits or very special Bourgognes that might qualify as a “cellar defender”, though I don’t look at them that way. The Mugneret version and Patrice Rion’s “Bons Batons” come most quickly to mind as “serious”, specific location Bourgognes. But, they are the exceptions. The rest hang in there with the Passetousgrains (a mixture of gamay and pinot noir that is also a “regional level wine”.

Bottom line: Bourgognes, IMO, are “unto themselves” and mostly unrelated in method, treatment and price to the rest of a domaine’s production. The villages/1er cru and grand cru are a separate category as a group at almost every domaine/negoce I know. Therefore, the bourgogne is not a good indicator of much more than what it is.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
Matthew,

You only paid $25 for this ? Domestic ?

posts.name: Carlos Delpin
posts.cooked:
Nick Gangas:

Matthew,

You only paid $25 for this ? Domestic ?

+1. Please do say where!

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Bottom line: Bourgognes, IMO, are “unto themselves” and mostly unrelated in method, treatment and price to the rest of a domaine’s production. The villages/1er cru and grand cru are a separate category as a group at almost every domaine/negoce I know. Therefore, the bourgogne is not a good indicator of much more than what it is.

Thanks Stuart – this is really helpful. I’ve often wondered about how bourgogne rouge is related to other wines in the stable of a major producer, and this structure makes a lot of sense. I would guess that there is a strong economic reason behind this structure as well.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Carlos Delpin:

Wally’s here in Los Angeles has been having an online Burgundy sale. You still might be able to find some Fourrier. I didn’t clear them out.

And thank you Stuart, John and others for thoughts on this topic. Makes sense.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
R. Frankel:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Bottom line: Bourgognes, IMO, are “unto themselves” and mostly unrelated in method, treatment and price to the rest of a domaine’s production. The villages/1er cru and grand cru are a separate category as a group at almost every domaine/negoce I know. Therefore, the bourgogne is not a good indicator of much more than what it is.

Thanks Stuart – this is really helpful. I’ve often wondered about how bourgogne rouge is related to other wines in the stable of a major producer, and this structure makes a lot of sense. I would guess that there is a strong economic reason behind this structure as well.

But, as John Stimson said, some domaines make all their wines pretty much the same way save, perhaps, for the amount of new oak. I couldn’t find any info on Fourrier, but Drouhin and Jadot’s sites suggest that the winemaking isn’t that different for the lesser wines. In those cases, I guess it’s not really right to say the Bourgognes are “unto themselves and mostly unrelated in method, treatment…”

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
I’ve never heard of anyone using new oak in the “winemaking” for a Bourgogne…or of anyone not using any new oak for their grand crus, John. That, alone, imo, makes them “unto themselves”…I also don’t know too many places (other than the two domaines I mentioned: Mugneret/Patrice Rion) who give their Bourgognes any elevage before bottling and selling.

I guess you could say “pretty much the same way” in that they are all made from pinot noir from Burgundy…or along many other criteria. But…it is pretty easy to find criteria that make them “not” the “same way”.

I don’t mean to suggest that places send their bourgogne grapes to the local supermarket for winemaking.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: Actually, these are side issues Stuart, but I think Chandon De Briailles uses very little new oak anywhere in it’s line up, including grand Cru’s. And I think there are a couple of estates that use a little new oak in their Bourgogne, but since I can’t remember who they are at the moment, don’t quote me on it.

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

I’ve never heard of anyone using new oak in the “winemaking” for a Bourgogne…or of anyone not using any new oak for their grand crus, John. That, alone, imo, makes them “unto themselves”…I also don’t know too many places (other than the two domaines I mentioned: Mugneret/Patrice Rion) who give their Bourgognes any elevage before bottling and selling.

I guess you could say “pretty much the same way” in that they are all made from pinot noir from Burgundy…or along many other criteria. But…it is pretty easy to find criteria that make them “not” the “same way”.

Stuart, I am told that Jean-Marie makes all the Fourrier wines the same way to highlight terroir expression. Having enjoyed his bourgognes many times, I wouldn’t be surprised if that held true at this level, too.

posts.name: paul clark
posts.cooked:
Difference between Regional and the rest starts with position on the slope. Regional wines tend to come from fruit grown on vines located on flat land where soils / loam tends to be much thicker, exposition to sun is poorer, humidity is higher, circulation of air is less, etc. The development of the fruit during the growing season is different. Even the best years the Regional fruit can be nicely ripe but they don’t develop the same structure which I relate to their less than ideal position in the valley versus up on the slope.

I drink quite a bit of Regional wines and I keep some around out of interest. The fruit tends to fade much more quickly. They get leaner earlier. After the intensity of the fruit calms down they tend to be on the lighter side. They age just fine but they tend to be shorter on the finish.

I cellar Ramonet, Barthod, Lignier and Lafarge. All of the wines see a year in oak and the treatment is similar if not identical to the treatment of the other wines (except they might be bottled earlier).

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Brady Daniels:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

I’ve never heard of anyone using new oak in the “winemaking” for a Bourgogne…or of anyone not using any new oak for their grand crus, John. That, alone, imo, makes them “unto themselves”…I also don’t know too many places (other than the two domaines I mentioned: Mugneret/Patrice Rion) who give their Bourgognes any elevage before bottling and selling.

I guess you could say “pretty much the same way” in that they are all made from pinot noir from Burgundy…or along many other criteria. But…it is pretty easy to find criteria that make them “not” the “same way”.

Stuart, I am told that Jean-Marie makes all the Fourrier wines the same way to highlight terroir expression. Having enjoyed his bourgognes many times, I wouldn’t be surprised if that held true at this level, too.

Most winemakers will tell you that: “the same way” for their lowest to highest wines. But, if you probe, it’s almost never the case. That the Bourgognes have left the domaine within a year…and the others are sitting in barrels for their elevage, awaiting bottling a year or so later is a significant difference in winemaking. And, except for two estates, I’ve never seen a Bourgogne in wood aging before bottling with the rest of the stable. Is that “the same way”??? I don’t think so.

That’s why I can’t look at regional wines as indicative of the rest of a domaine’s hierarchy. I suppose it can give some insight into a vintage, and I do look at it that way, though I buy them to drink …and mainly/exclusively have bought the ones I know are well made: Mugneret, Patrice Rion Bons Batons, Robert Chevillon. I know they are likely to be good wines, even if not necessarily reflective of the maturity or potential of the rest of a particular stable in a given year. Most other bourgognes I am familiar with are , IMO, different animals…that are rarely offered for tasting during a visit. (And, that tells me something, especially when I have asked to taste their bourgognes and been declined.)

fancy_title: Fourrier and question about Bourgogne Rouge
title: Fourrier and question about Bourgogne Rouge
